persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter-case: 6 Musson biographies and 5 Musson officials exist, so a same-name relative (the surname is shared by the blocking key) could in principle hold a Jamaica clerkship. This fails because the entire distinctive career chain matches point-for-point in matching years: the bio's 'first-class clerk, colonial secretary's office, Oct., 1872' equals the stint's '1st Class Clerks, Colonial Secretary's Office' (1877-83); 'chief clerk, 1884' equals the stint's 'Chief Clerk, Colonial Secretary's Office' (1886-96); 'treas., Jamaica, 1896' equals 'Public Treasurer, Treasury' (1898-1900); 'nominated mem. of legis. coun.' equals 'Island Treasurer, Legislative Council' (1898). Five agreeing edition-years (1886-1894) with position similarity ~100 corroborate. No relative would replicate this exact senior-office progression in the same office and years.

evidence_cited.fact: first-class clerk, colonial secretary's office, Oct., 1872

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a distinct second S. P. Musson clerking in the same Jamaica Colonial Secretary's Office across these years would reverse this.

notes: Detailed career continuity (clerk to chief clerk to treasurer to legislative council) plus 5 agreeing edition-years. High-confidence match.
